mūla.devanāgarī: किम्-अर्थम् ? परित्राणाय साधूनां विनाशाय च दुष्कृताम् | धर्म-संस्थापनार्थाय संभवामि युगे युगे
mūla.iast: kim-artham ? paritrāṇāya sādhūnāṃ vināśāya ca duṣkṛtām | dharma-saṃsthāpanārthāya saṃbhavāmi yuge yuge
mūla.chapter_position: Chapter 4 (Jñāna-Karma-Sannyāsa-Yoga (The Yoga of Renunciation of Action in Knowledge)), verse 8
mūla.speaker: Krishna

senses_attested_in_panel.sense: साधूनां इति साधुपरित्राणादिकं भगवदवतारस्य प्रयोजनमुक्तम् तत्र किं जन्मनैव परित्राणादिकं कार्यमिति नियमः इत्यपेक्षायामाह
तत्प्रतिपक्षाणां दुष्कृतां दुष्टं कर्म कुर्वतां विनाशाय
परितः सर्वतो रक्षणाय तथा अधर्मवृद्ध्या वर्धमानानां दुष्कृतां पापकारिणां वेदमार्गविरोधिनां विनाशाय
च दुष्कृतां पापकारिणाम् किञ्च धर्मसंस्थापनार्थाय धर्मस्य सम्यक् स्थापनं तदर्थं संभवामि युगे युगे प्रतियुगम्

advaita.reading_summary: (reading summary extraction pending; ENABLE_READING_SUMMARIES=true to generate)
advaita.english_rendering: For the protection (paritrāṇa) of those established in the right path (sanmārga-stha) and the destruction of the sinful (pāpakārin), Īśvara — who in reality undergoes no true birth — appears age after age through māyā. The third purpose subsumes both: by removing adharma and reinstating Vedic dharma, the apparent Lord restores the very conditions under which jñāna can arise in qualified seekers. The avatāra is thus not a cosmic rescue but an epistemological clearing — the real work remains the seeker's own discrimination.
advaita.divergence_note: parirakṣaṇāya sanmārgasthānām ... dharmasya samyak sthāpanaṃ tadartham

viśiṣṭādvaita.reading_summary: (reading summary extraction pending; ENABLE_READING_SUMMARIES=true to generate)
viśiṣṭādvaita.english_rendering: The sādhus here are specifically the Vaiṣṇava devotees (mat-samāśrayana-pravṛtta) who, unable to bear even a moment's separation from Bhagavān's form and deeds (nāma-karma-svarūpa), become undone in body and limb — for their paritrāṇa (rescue from that unbearable longing), Bhagavān descends with his form, speech, and glance as nourishment. The duṣkṛtas are not merely sinners but those opposed to this devotional dharma; their destruction too serves the bhaktas' welfare. Descent is an act of love in every age, unrestricted to any particular yuga.
viśiṣṭādvaita.divergence_note: aṇumātrakālam api kalpasahasraṃ manvānāḥ praśithilasarvagātrā ... mat-svarūpa-ceṣṭitāvalokana-alāpādi-dānena teṣāṃ paritrāṇāya

dvaita.reading_summary: (reading summary extraction pending; ENABLE_READING_SUMMARIES=true to generate)
dvaita.english_rendering: There is no rule that birth alone accomplishes protection and destruction; Hari acts by his own svabhāva (intrinsic nature) and by līlā (spontaneous play), entirely free from compulsion. Like a child at sport — yet the child is Anantavīrya, of boundless power — he who is pūrṇa (utterly full) and has nothing to gain still undertakes all action; the seers who know the highest and the lowest (parāvara-jña) declare him to act out of apparent contradiction, which is itself the proof of his absolute independence (svātantrya). Paritrāṇa and vināśa are the sport of the supremely full One.
dvaita.divergence_note: na janmanaiva paritrāṇādi kāryam iti niyamaḥ ... līlayā svabhāvena ca yatheiṣṭacārī ... pūrṇo'yam asya na kiñcid āpyam tathāpi sarvāḥ kurute pravṛttīḥ

śuddhādvaita.reading_summary: (reading summary extraction pending; ENABLE_READING_SUMMARIES=true to generate)
śuddhādvaita.english_rendering: Verse 4.7 named the when; 4.8 names the purpose — but in Puṣṭi-mārga the tripartite purpose is entirely Kṛṣṇa's own prasāda-impulse: he protects the dharma-vat (those who possess dharma), destroys the duṣkṛt (those whose karma is corrupt), and does so yuge yuge without calendrical constraint. Even the apparent severity of destruction carries no pitilessness (nairghṛṇya): as a mother who both caresses and strikes her infant acts without cruelty, so Maheśa — the supreme controller of virtue and fault — acts solely from niyantṛ-love. The descent is both when-he-wills and how-he-wills.
śuddhādvaita.divergence_note: nāvatārakālaniyama ity etadarthaṃ yuge yuge uktam ... lālane tāḍane mātur nākāruṇyaṃ yathārbhake

bhakti.reading_summary: (reading summary extraction pending; ENABLE_READING_SUMMARIES=true to generate)
bhakti.english_rendering: The question 'for what purpose?' (kim-artham) is answered in three clean steps: protection of the sādhus (those who walk svadharma), destruction of the duṣkṛtas (evil-doers), and through both acts together the firm establishment of dharma (dharmaṃ sthīrīkartum). Śrīdhara, like Vallabha, adds the mother-analogy to remove any charge of cruelty against the destroyer-function. The descent is occasioned not by yuga-sequence but by the arising of the need (tat-tad-avasare) — an on-demand, responsive avatāra theology.
bhakti.divergence_note: sādhurakṣaṇena duṣṭavadhena ca dharmaṃ sthīrīkartuṃ yuge yuge tattad-avasare sambhavāmīty arthaḥ

advaita-bhakti.reading_summary: (reading summary extraction pending; ENABLE_READING_SUMMARIES=true to generate)
advaita-bhakti.english_rendering: Madhusūdana opens with a dialectical challenge: would not the very occasion of dharma's decline and adharma's rise — the pretext for avatāra — make the descent itself a source of harm? He refutes this by unpacking the three purposes as a single coherent act: paritrāṇa means protecting from every side (paritaḥ sarvato rakṣaṇam) the sādhus on the Vedic path now imperiled; vināśa removes those actively opposing that path; and dharma-saṃsthāpana is precisely the preservation of veda-mārga by the removal of adharma. The avatāra is therapeutically complete — diagnosis, excision, and restoration in one descent.
advaita-bhakti.divergence_note: paritrāṇāya paritaḥ sarvato rakṣaṇāya ... dharmasya samyag adharma-nivāraṇena sthāpanaṃ veda-mārga-parirakṣaṇaṃ dharmasaṃsthāpanam

corpus_provenance.mūla: Belvalkar critical edition (BORI 1947), via Ambuda multi-witness

post_generation_repairs.fix: verb-lemma-misidentification (broader heuristic: prefix-√root canonical for all verb-tagged tokens)

so_what_questions: If avatāra is māyā-mediated (Advaita) rather than a real birth, what moral weight does its protective-destructive function carry — can an unreal agent truly protect or destroy?
Rāmānuja defines the sādhus as devotees in acute separation-longing: does this restrict the promise of paritrāṇa to advanced bhaktas, or does it simply reveal the deepest form of the general promise?
Madhva insists descent is pure līlā with nothing to gain — yet the verse lists three purposes. How does Dvaita reconcile purposive language with the doctrine of Hari's absolute pūrṇatva?

All six schools agree 'yuge yuge' means no fixed-yuga rule. What does an on-demand, occasion-responsive avatāra theology imply for communities living through apparent periods of adharma without a visible descent?

The dharma-saṃsthāpana purpose is shared across schools yet filled with different content: Vedic path (Śaṅkara, Madhusūdana), Vaiṣṇava bhakti-dharma (Rāmānuja), svadharma-observance (Śrīdhara). Whose dharma is being established?

Vallabha and Śrīdhara both invoke the mother-analogy to neutralize the nairghṛṇya (cruelty) charge against vināśa. Does this analogy dissolve the moral problem or merely relocate it into a relationship of asymmetric power?

If protection and destruction are two faces of a single dharma-restoring act, is there a reading of this verse in which the duṣkṛta is also, in some sense, being served — freed from the karma that was destroying them?

everyday_applications.advaita: When you take on a protective or corrective role — parenting, teaching, managing — act cleanly from dharma, not from emotional investment in outcomes. Śaṅkara's avatāra clears conditions for jñāna; your interventions should clear conditions for others' clarity, then step back. The moment you believe you are the rescuer, you have mistaken the instrument for the source.
everyday_applications.viśiṣṭādvaita: When someone you love is in acute longing or suffering — the Rāmānuja sādhu model — your 'descent' is to show up with presence, story, and voice (svarūpa-ceṣṭita-āvalokanā), not with solutions. The care that relieves devotional-style longing is relational and sensory, not merely transactional. Schedule the visit, make the call; paritrāṇa here is showing your face.
everyday_applications.dvaita: Act from your deepest svabhāva (innate nature) rather than from need for recognition or fear of failure — Madhva's Hari acts because it is his nature, not because he lacks anything. Identify the work that flows from your nature effortlessly (as the child plays), and do it fully. The fact that you have nothing to gain is the condition, not the obstacle.
everyday_applications.śuddhādvaita: In a community or family role, trust that corrective action — even firm, uncomfortable correction — is not a betrayal of love if it flows from the niyantṛ (governing) function. The mother who disciplines is not cruel. Vallabha's frame: the sign of authentic care is that both the lālana (nurturing) and the tāḍana (correction) come from the same undivided intention.
everyday_applications.bhakti: Recognize that your interventions — in an organization, a neighborhood, a family system — become dharma-saṃsthāpana only when protection of the good and removal of the harmful are coordinated, not pursued separately. Śrīdhara's reading: neither sādhu-rakṣaṇa alone nor duṣṭa-vadha alone establishes dharma — both must be present, occasion-responsive, and proportionate.
everyday_applications.advaita-bhakti: When entering a situation of visible decay — an institution losing its dharma, a community fraying — adopt Madhusūdana's therapeutic completeness: diagnose what is threatened (sādhus), identify what is actively corrosive (duṣkṛtas), and focus your energy on restoring the underlying structure (veda-mārga, the generative principle). Partial interventions that protect without removing the corrosive element, or remove without restoring the structure, are incomplete descents.

primary_meaning: I come into being age after age to protect the righteous, destroy evildoers, and set *dharma* firmly back on its feet.
